Last meeting I went flat with a mild flattening lean (curve_slope -0.20, term_premium +0.05, balance_sheet -0.75 QT continuing). This meeting the evidence has actually sharpened, and it points more clearly toward steepening pressure than my prior read assumed. Term_premium now reports a genuine upward drift in DGS10 (+0.36pp/21d, +0.63pp/63d, +1.16pp/252d, near top of 12m range) with conviction rising to 0.45 from 0.05 — this is a live, near-term repricing signal on the long end, not a stale one. Balance_sheet remains firmly down (-0.78 conv) with QT continuing at pace and no taper signal — this is a slow, well-anticipated structural driver that's already priced into term premium; it corroborates the long-end-supply story but shouldn't be weighted for its own timing value. Curve_slope itself reports a choppy, whipsawing 2s10s (-0.35, near middle of range) with only 0.30 conviction and an admitted reversal of its own prior call — this is the least trustworthy read this round given internal inconsistency. Given the level is now meaningfully above the recent low and off natural-rate anchor, and there's no sign of reversal back toward 4.00% or below, I lean toward continued softness but with reduced conviction versus a clean upward call, since the immediate momentum has stalled.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 4.00% or below, or a third consecutive flat print at 4.20%, would confirm stabilization rather than continued loosening.

arbitrated.notes:
Reconciling for 2s10s slope. My prior call was a modest steepener bias (curve_slope +0.20, term_premium +0.55, labor_tightness +0.35, balance_sheet -0.62). This meeting's evidence still points steepening but with weaker long-end conviction than before, so I keep direction but temper size.

Direct slope evidence: curve_slope itself reports the spread pulled back over the last two prints (0.20→0.15) after a steepening run, but multi-horizon momentum (21d/63d/252d all positive, range position 0.86) still favors steepening resuming. That's the most load-bearing input for a slope call and it stays net "up" (steepening), just with tempered conviction (0.35, down from what would have been a stronger read).

Front-end: labor_tightness ticked down to 4.10% from 4.20%, a partial reversal of the loosening trend, 3m change now flat. This is a front-end input — a stabilizing labor market reduces the odds of aggressive near-term cuts, which would compress front-end declines and could cap steepening from that channel. It's a modest headwind to the steepening thesis versus last meeting, but still weak/ambiguous (flat call, low conviction).

Long-end: term_premium downgraded from up (0.45) to flat (0.25) — the dominant 63d/252d momentum in DGS10 is still negative, yield near 12m lows, arguing against a term-premium-driven long-end selloff. This undercuts the long-end-steepening channel somewhat. Balance_sheet (down/QT continuing, 0.68 conviction) is a slow-moving, well-anticipated supply factor already priced into term premium expectations — I discount its incremental trading signal for the month-ahead horizon, though its is directionally consistent with a firmer long end (steepening support via less anchoring pressure, but genuinely marginal).

Net: steepening bias persists but weaker than previously — the primary slope indicator itself is decelerating, and the term premium engine that would drive long-end-led steepening has cooled. I hold a modest steepener, not sized up, consistent with holding my position but not adding given the softened evidence.

arbitrated.notes: Holding a steepener but sizing down conviction slightly given fresh evidence of stalling momentum. The core case for 2s10s steepening remains intact structurally: labor_tightness has plateaued (unrate flat at 4.10%, 3m change now improving) rather than tightening further, and the Fed's just-delivered 50bp cut plus dovish reaction function keeps front-end yields anchored/declining as cuts continue — this is the dominant near-term repricing force on the curve, more than slow-moving balance-sheet or NFCI trends which are largely priced. curve_slope itself confirms the medium-term steepening trend intact (63d/252d changes strongly positive, range_position 0.82) but flags a real wobble: slope_2s10s pulled back to 0.12 from 0.17, with 21d change flat-to-slightly negative. This hasn't breached my prior flip condition (below 0.05 with negative 21d momentum) but it is a genuine loss of near-term momentum that argues against adding to the position. term_premium reports the long end has plateaued near 4.28% after its climb, deceleration in the 21d/63d impulse — consistent with a stalling steepener rather than a reversal, since long-end yields aren't pushing higher aggressively even as balance-sheet runoff continues (a slow, well-anticipated supply story, not a fresh catalyst). Inflation data (up, reaccelerating CPI/core PCE) is a two-sided risk: it could pressure long-end term premium up (steepening) but could also delay cuts and flatten via front-end (labor market resilience risk). Net, I keep the steepener but trim conviction to reflect the stalled slope momentum and offsetting inflation crosscurrents — this is a case for patience, not reversal, since no falsifier condition was actually triggered this meeting.

arbitrated.notes:
Last meeting I was long a steepener (short 2y / long 10y) on the view that balance-sheet runoff plus a genuine steepening trend would push long yields up relative to front yields. This meeting's evidence undercuts that. The curve_slope analyst's own call flipped from "up" to "down" — the 2s10s spread has fallen to 0.05 with clear negative 21d momentum, explicitly triggering the falsifier I set last time. That is a direct, on-seat signal that the flattening move I was betting against is now the live trend, not the steepening continuation I had positioned for.

Cross-checking against the front-vs-long balance: labor_tightness printed a fresh cycle high (4.20%, at top of range), which is a genuine loosening-labor signal — dovish for the front end, arguing for continued/eventual cuts and front-end anchoring or richening, which is flattening-supportive if the long end doesn't move proportionally. Inflation (up, conviction 0.55) is the freshest, most trade-relevant driver and argues for higher realized/expected inflation, which usually keeps term premium and long-end yields firm — but that's a level effect, not necessarily a steepening one, since a hot CPI print with capped Fed cuts (curve_slope analyst notes smaller 1/4-point cuts) also supports front-end stickiness. Term_premium's own call (down, conviction 0.40) suggests long-end yields pulling back toward 4.15, which is flattening-consistent given the front end isn't falling in tandem (labor is tight, inflation is up). Balance-sheet runoff (down, 0.75 conviction, very confident) is a slow, well-anticipated, well-priced driver — high conviction on it doesn't translate to slope-moving information; I downweight it accordingly despite its confidence, per the general framework.

Net: the actual slope measurement broke to my stated flip level with negative momentum, term premium is easing at the long end, and labor/inflation dynamics support front-end stickiness rather than front-end collapse. This reconciles to a flattening call, reversing my prior steepener.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a steepener (long DGS10, short DGS2) on the back of a shrinking balance sheet pushing term premium up and a curve_slope reading that was leaning positive. This meeting largely undercuts that thesis on the long end. Term_premium has flipped from "up" to "down" with 0.45 conviction — the 10y fell to 4.24 from 4.47, a clear -0.31pp 21d move, breaching its own falsifier level. That's a direct long-end repricing lower, which flattens 2s10s given a stable front end. Curve_slope itself has gone from +0.35 (up) to essentially flat/slight-steepening-residual at 0.25 conviction, having stalled and pulled back from 0.27 toward 0.20-0.25 range — its own falsifier condition (break below 0.28 with negative slope_change_21d) is effectively triggered. Balance_sheet remains a confident "down" (0.75) but this is a slow, well-anticipated structural driver already priced into term premium — I discount its incremental trade signal versus the live repricing in term_premium itself. Labor_tightness is flat/choppy (range-bound 3.9-4.2%), giving no fresh front-end steepening or flattening impulse; unemployment ticked up but within noise band, arguing against front-end selloff that would resist flattening. Given the long end has just repriced down in yield with no offsetting front-end move, and my own steepener falsifier conditions have essentially fired (2s10s pulling back, 10y reversing below 4.45), I am reversing toward a flattener stance, modestly sized given the slope call itself is only "flat" with a slight steepening residual and choppy, not a clean trend.

With the trend intact, no break above 4.40, and reduced runoff-driven supply pressure, I lean toward continued softness or stabilization in the term premium, though the pace of decline has decelerated (21d change is small), suggesting the move may be flattening out near current levels.

This is a substantial slowing of the pace of QT (potential total cap runoff falls from up to $60B/month to as low as $40B/month, though actual paydowns are often below caps). The direction remains runoff (down) — assets should still decline into month-end — but the recent 4-week and 13-week pace (~$9-30B/month) already looks close to or even below the new combined cap ceiling, so continued decline is likely but at a decidedly slower rate than the trailing 52-week pace (-611,075 $M) would suggest. I maintain a "down" call for next month-end, but with reduced conviction given the explicit policy shift toward a much slower runoff pace, and note Waller's dissent shows the taper itself was contested, underscoring this is a genuine regime change, not just noise.

arbitrated.notes:
Carrying a 2s10s steepener (long DGS10 / short DGS2) from last meeting on the back of curve_slope+, balance_sheet reduction, and soft term_premium. This meeting's panel keeps the steepening thesis intact but trims conviction rather than reversing it.

Curve_slope itself: the analyst downgrades conviction from 0.40 to 0.30, flagging that the falsifier partially triggered (21d change went negative) but the level held at 0.51-0.52, well above the 0.50 break and far from the 0.48 reversal trigger. This is stalling, not reversal — still a modest "up" (steepening) call. I weight this heavily since it's the direct slope read.

Front end: labor_tightness is the key repricing driver here — unemployment broke to a fresh 12-month high (4.30%), a genuine trend break (not just noise) with policy language shifting toward acknowledging employment risk. A loosening labor market pulls front-end yields down as cut expectations build, which is steepening-supportive on its own (front end anchored/falling while long end sticky). This is a live, near-term reaction-function driver, so I weight it high despite modest conviction.

Long end: term_premium comes in flat (0.30 conviction, downgraded from prior implicit "down" stance) — yields still elevated in upper-range, slope 30s10s steep, but the recent pullback and slowing QT cap are mild disinflationary-for-term-premium forces. Balance_sheet confirms continued runoff but at a decisively slower pace (cap cut $25B→$5B) — reduced long-end supply pressure is a modest cap on long-end yield rises, but the analyst still calls "down" direction with reduced conviction (0.45→lower effective read). Net long-end signal is closer to neutral-to-mild-support for steepening (slower runoff = less pressure pushing yields up further, but not compressing rapidly).

Synthesis: front-end loosening labor + long-end term premium plateau/mild deceleration in QT both favor continued modest steepening, but with materially lower conviction than last month given curve_slope's own stall and term_premium's flat call. I hold the position but shrink size/conviction.

discounted.why: Slow-moving, largely priced policy shift; directionally supportive but not a fresh repricing catalyst this month.
Flat call itself, and lacks balance_sheet/financial_conditions inputs per its own admission — thin basis for long-end view.

arbitrated.falsifier: A slope_2s10s break below 0.48 with 21d change turning sharply negative, or labor_tightness reversing back to 4.1-4.2%, would flip this back toward flat/flattener.

risks.text: Curve_slope's own momentum has stalled; a further plateau could erase the steepening edge entirely.
Slowing QT taper could be read as long-end supportive (lower supply), capping steepening from the long end.
Position is a legacy holding; conviction has fallen versus last meeting and may not justify full size.
Labor loosening could also just pull the whole curve down in parallel if a policy pivot dominates, muddying slope read.

trade.rationale: Trim prior 0.50/0.50 steepener to 0.35/0.35 given curve_slope's stall and term_premium going flat; still hold direction as labor loosening supports front-end anchoring while long end holds firmer.

why.curve_slope: Spread stalled at 0.51-0.52, near range highs; momentum stalling but no clean reversal below 0.48-0.50 trigger.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment broke to fresh 12m high (4.30%), genuine loosening trend, not just noise; front-end repricing driver.
why.term_premium: Yields elevated but pulling back modestly; slope flat call, no strong direction on long-end term premium.
why.balance_sheet: Runoff continues (down) but pace decisively slowing under new $5B cap; less long-end supply pressure than before.

user_prompt:
Your previous meeting
  You called: balance_sheet -0.30, curve_slope +0.30, labor_tightness +0.50, term_premium +0.05
  The position you are carrying: DGS10 +0.35, DGS2 -0.35 (conviction 0.35).
  You said this read would flip if: A slope_2s10s break below 0.48 with 21d change turning sharply negative, or labor_tightness reversing back to 4.1-4.2%, would flip this back toward flat/flattener.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (6,662,200) fell sharply from the prior week (6,681,056), a drop of ~18,856M in a single week — larger than the trailing 4-week change of -11,044M, confirming the runoff is still active and even accelerating somewhat versus the immediately preceding weeks. The 13-week trend remains firmly negative (-78,053M, -1.16%), and assets sit well below the 52-week high (-561,879M). There was some noisy sideways/up movement a few observations back (e.g., 6,673,244→6,672,885→6,677,155→6,681,056 showed a brief uptick), which had tempered my prior conviction, but the most recent observation reverses that bounce decisively to a new series low. Policy language is unchanged: continued balance-sheet reduction (QT) with no signal of tapering or halting caps. There is no evidence in front of me of runoff flattening toward zero — the opposite, a fresh series low, is what I see. Given the announced caps and the plain confirmation of continued decline in the newest data point, I maintain a down call, and with slightly higher conviction than before since the most recent print resolved the intra-month noise in the direction consistent with ongoing QT.
Would change its mind if: A weekly rise in fed_assets, or confirmation that actual runoff has flattened to near-zero under current caps.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print shows a pullback: slope_2s10s eased from 0.56 to 0.52 after five straight prints in the 0.55-0.56 range, giving the newest reading a small down-tick. My prior "up" call was directionally right through most of the window (spread rose from 0.44-0.49 base to the mid-0.50s, slope_change_252d +0.94pp, slope_change_63d +0.14pp) but the most recent single-period move is a modest reversal, not a confirmed break — slope_change_21d is still slightly positive (+0.01pp), well above the -0.10pp threshold I set for calling a genuine reversal. So the flattening signal from the last data point is real but weak and could easily be noise/curve-normalization pause after a steep run. The spread remains near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.86, only 0.15pp below the 12m high of 0.67) and 0.93pp above the 12m low, so the dominant one-year trend is still steepening from deeply inverted territory. Given the recent tick-down, I'll moderate conviction and shade toward flat-to-slightly-up rather than repeating a firm "up" call, since momentum has clearly stalled even if the reversal isn't confirmed.
Would change its mind if: A further drop below 0.48 with slope_change_21d turning below -0.10pp would confirm a genuine flattening reversal rather than a pause.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a persistent, steady easing trend: from -0.41 down to -0.49 over 13 weeks, with the most recent prints (-0.45, -0.46, -0.47, -0.48, -0.49) showing consistent, unbroken monthly declines. The 4-week change (-0.03) and 13-week change (-0.06) both confirm continued easing momentum, and the pace of decline in the most recent weeks looks slightly faster than the trailing 13-week average would suggest, since the last five readings alone account for half of the 13-week move. The index sits at -0.49, near its 52-week low of -0.51 (only 0.02 above it) and far below the zero average, meaning conditions are already loose and getting looser. Range position of 0.12 (out of 1) confirms the index is near the bottom of its 52-week range. My prior call (down) was correct — there was no reversal above -0.40, and the 4-week change remained negative throughout. With no offsetting signal in the latest statement and momentum still pointing lower, I expect continued easing into month-end, though the pace of decline is modest and could stall as the index approaches new lows.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above -0.45 with a flat-to-positive 4-week change would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_above_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has stabilized/drifted slightly lower, not higher. The series shows a peak of 2.33 mid-window, then a decline to 2.28-2.29, where it has held for the last three readings. be_change_21d (-0.03) and be_change_63d (-0.08) are both negative, while be_change_252d remains positive (+0.07), indicating the longer-term uptrend has stalled and reversed modestly over the past one to three months. The level has settled at 2.29, comfortably within the 12-month range (61st percentile) and 0.29pp above the 2% target — not extreme, but not falling toward target either. The last three prints (2.28, 2.28, 2.29) show flattening rather than continued decline, suggesting the recent drop has found a floor. Given the negative short and medium-term momentum but the apparent stabilization in the very latest readings, I see this as essentially flat with a slight downward tilt rather than a resumption of the prior uptrend. My falsifier condition (21d change turning negative) has now been met, which per my own criteria reverts this call away from "up."
Would change its mind if: A move back above 2.32 sustained for multiple readings, or be_change_21d turning positive again, would revert this to an up call.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The last print rose to 2.68% y/y headline from 2.38% prior month, with headline_mom jumping to 0.25% from 0.10% — a clear reacceleration in the monthly pace, though not above my 0.30% falsifier threshold. Core PCE ticked up to 2.78% from 2.61%, also below the 2.9% rebound threshold I'd flagged, so neither trigger was strictly hit, but both moved in the accelerating direction. My prior "flat" call undershot this bounce: headline_yoy_change_3m is now +0.30pp, confirming upward momentum over the recent quarter even as core_yoy_change_3m is still -0.18pp (base effects still working through core). The 3m annualized rates for both headline and core sit at 2.08%, right near target-consistent pace, which tempers alarm — the underlying trend isn't runaway, but the single-month jump in mom is notable and outgoing_mom equals the 3m average, suggesting the higher pace is not just a one-off outlier. Given headline is coming off a 12m-range low reading two months prior and has now bounced meaningfully, I lean toward another firm or slightly higher headline print next month, driven partly by base effects unwinding. Core remains the better persistence signal and is only modestly elevated, so I don't see a sharp acceleration, just a mild upward drift.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.10% or a drop in headline yoy back toward 2.3-2.4% would indicate the bounce was noise, not trend.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.10% from 4.30%, undershooting my falsifier threshold (4.2% or below) that I flagged as "noise" territory. My prior 'up' call at 0.45 conviction was wrong for this release — the rate did not continue rising, it reversed sharply. The 13-month series shows a bouncy but essentially range-bound pattern (4.00-4.30%) with no sustained trend: unrate_change_6m and unrate_change_12m are both flat at 0.00pp, and the 3m change is now -0.10pp. Sahm-rule spirit gap is a modest 0.20pp, well below recession-signal thresholds, and unrate_range_position at 0.33 shows the rate sits in the lower third of its recent range, not the upper end. The level (4.10%) is only 0.10pp above my assumed natural rate, essentially at equilibrium. Policy language has shifted to flag two-sided risk (higher unemployment now explicitly mentioned alongside inflation), which warrants some caution, but the unchanged language still calls conditions "solid" and "stabilized at a low level." Given the whipsaw within a narrow band and a fresh move back to the cycle's tighter end, I read this as broadly flat with a slight loosening risk bias given the new two-sided risk language.
Would change its mind if: A print at/above 4.3% would signal renewed loosening; a print at/below 4.0% would confirm continued tightening beyond noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year has drifted lower over the last 10 readings, from 4.46 to 4.24, with the most recent print making a fresh local low. The 21-day change of -0.19pp shows clear recent downward momentum, though the 63-day (-0.03pp) and 252-day (+0.01pp) changes show this is a fairly recent development rather than a sustained multi-month trend. My prior flat call is now looking wrong on the recent print: yields broke below the 4.35 threshold I had flagged as a trigger for a down call, and did so with the range position sitting at 0.53 (still mid-range, not extreme) but with the trajectory clearly pointing down. The 30s10s slope at 0.54pp has not meaningfully flattened, so the confirmation is partial, not full. Still, continued balance-sheet runoff should, per the standing framework, exert some upward pressure on long-end supply/premium, yet the measured yield has moved the other way, suggesting other forces (perhaps softer growth/inflation expectations or flight-to-quality flows) are dominating for now. Given the clear recent momentum lower and the break of my own stated trigger level, I am shifting from flat to a modest down call, though I temper conviction since the slope didn't flatten as much as I'd want and the longer-window changes are flat-to-slightly-up.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.35 with 30s10s steepening further would indicate the recent decline was noise, not a genuine premium shift.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/composition of runoff and Treasury issuance guidance would clarify supply pressure on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify if recent yield drop reflects flight-to-quality.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a steepener (long DGS10, short DGS2, conviction 0.35) built on: curve_slope trending up, labor_tightness rising (tightening reversing → higher yields/less front-end anchoring), balance_sheet shrinking (long-end supply pressure), and a benign term_premium. This meeting's panel meaningfully undercuts that setup. Curve_slope itself has ticked down (0.56→0.52) and the analyst downgraded conviction to 0.25 flat, though the 21d change is still marginally positive — not a confirmed reversal, but momentum has clearly stalled. More importantly, labor_tightness reversed sharply (4.30%→4.10%), directly hitting my own stated falsifier threshold (4.1-4.2% flip zone) from last meeting — that driver no longer supports a steepening bias from the front end; if anything it argues for a flatter curve as tightening fears fade. Term_premium flipped from flat to a fresh "down" call, with 10y yields breaking below the analyst's own 4.35 trigger and making fresh local lows — this is a live, freshly-formed long-end signal that directly cuts against the steepener thesis: falling long-end yields with a stalling slope reading point toward flattening pressure, not further steepening. Balance_sheet remains reliably down (shrinking further), which is the one holdover still consistent with long-end supply pressure, but it's a slow-moving, well-anticipated variable with limited near-term repricing power compared to the live labor and term-premium reversals. Given two of my four original pillars (labor_tightness, term_premium) have now turned or hit their falsifiers, and curve_slope momentum has stalled, I no longer see sufficient evidence to hold the steepener at prior conviction. I'm cutting the position size materially while retaining a small residual steepening tilt given balance_sheet and the still-positive 12-month slope trend, rather than flipping outright to a flattener on a single month's noisy prints.

discounted.why: Slow, well-anticipated driver; supply pressure thesis intact but low near-term repricing power versus live reversals elsewhere.

arbitrated.falsifier: A slope_2s10s break below 0.48 with 21d change below -0.10pp, or labor_tightness confirming below 4.0%, would flip this to an outright flattener.

risks.text: Slope's 21d change is still marginally positive; the flattening signal could be noise, leaving me under-positioned on a real steepener.
Term_premium's long-end decline could reflect flight-to-quality rather than a genuine premium shift, reversing quickly if risk sentiment stabilizes.
Labor reversal is a single print in a range-bound series; a rebound above 4.3% would revive the tightening/steepening thesis.
Cutting size into a stale but still-directional balance sheet signal risks missing continued long-end supply-driven steepening.

trade.rationale: Trim steepener sharply: two of four prior pillars (labor, term premium) have reversed or hit falsifiers this meeting. Retain small residual steepening tilt on balance-sheet runoff and still-positive 12m slope trend, not a full flip to flattener.

why.curve_slope: Slope ticked down to 0.52 from 0.56; 21d change barely positive, momentum stalled, not confirmed reversal — trim from prior +0.30.
why.labor_tightness: Unrate fell back to 4.10%, hitting my own falsifier zone; tightening thesis reversed, no longer supports steepener.
why.balance_sheet: Runoff accelerated to fresh series low, still exerting long-end supply pressure; hold prior down call but low weight given slow/priced nature.
why.term_premium: 10y yields broke below own 4.35 trigger to fresh lows; live reversal from flat, cuts directly against steepener, most trade-relevant of the four.

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The slope has continued to compress, extending the flattening move I flagged as a risk last time. Latest prints: 0.51, 0.48, 0.44, 0.43 — a clear step-down over the last four readings, not noise. My prior "flat" call underweighted this; the 21d change (-0.05pp) understates the recent acceleration since most of that decline is concentrated in just the last two observations (0.48→0.44→0.43, i.e., -0.05pp in two prints alone). The 63d change (-0.14pp) confirms a multi-week flattening trend, even though the 252d change (+0.59pp) shows the curve is still far steeper than a year ago and well off its 12m low (range position 0.72, i.e., still in the upper part of the 12-month range). So this is a partial retracement of a prior steepening, not a return to deep inversion. Dgs2 and dgs10 levels aren't my domain, but the spread math shows short-end yields rising faster than long-end. Given the consistent multi-print downtrend and breach of my own falsifier threshold (below 0.48, with declining momentum), I now lean toward continued flattening into month-end, though conviction is moderate given the curve remains well within positive, non-inverted territory and 10s30s (+0.52pp) shows the long end itself isn't inverting.

The deceleration in the 4-week pace (-0.02 vs -0.13 over 13 weeks, implying roughly -0.03 to -0.04 per 4-week block earlier) suggests the rate of easing may be flattening, tempering conviction slightly versus outright continuation, but the directional signal remains firmly toward further easing at month-end.

But labor_tightness also turned — unemployment rate drifting up to 4.30% (12m high), a loosening labor market that typically drives front-end yields down as the market prices earlier/more Fed cuts. Front-end easing expectations plus long-end term-premium firmness is the textbook combination for curve steepening at the front (short yields falling faster than long), which contradicts the raw slope_2s10s print showing compression.

This is exactly the disagreement the seat is built to reconcile: the measured 2s10s spread is compressing, but the fundamental drivers (front-end labor-driven cuts pricing, long-end term premium firming) argue front-end should fall faster than long end — i.e., steepening pressure building beneath a currently-flattening tape. I weight the direct, fresh curve_slope print heavily since it's the target variable itself and monotonic across four prints, but I temper the flattening call given labor_tightness's loosening signal cuts against it. Balance_sheet (still down, QT continuing) is a slow, priced-in, long-end supply story — directionally steepening-supportive but low near-term repricing power. arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a modest steepener (long DGS10, short DGS2, conviction 0.40) built on curve_slope momentum, labor loosening, and QT continuing to hit long-end supply. This meeting's panel materially undercuts that setup. curve_slope itself flipped from "up 0.45" to "flat 0.25" — its own falsifier (break below 0.55 with negative 21d change) essentially triggered before bouncing back to 0.56, so the analyst now describes a choppy, directionless 0.52-0.59 band rather than a trend. That's the most direct evidence against my prior steepener and it comes from the driver I trade off of. term_premium also softened to flat/low-conviction (0.20), with the 10y stalling right at its threshold — no fresh long-end pressure. balance_sheet reversed from confident "down 0.65" to "flat 0.35," conceding a 4-week rebound in assets even though the multi-month QT trend nominally continues — this is a slow, well-anticipated driver anyway and I discount it further per the mandate to favor live reaction-function signals over balance-sheet drift. labor_tightness firmed to "up 0.45" (unemployment rising, sahm gap widening), which is front-end relevant: a loosening labor market raises rate-cut odds, pulling the front end down more than the long end — mechanically a steepening impulse on the rate axis. But inflation printed "up 0.55" with headline at a 12-month high and accelerating, which pushes back against front-end cuts and would flatten via the front end selling off less / long end anchored by inflation risk premium being capped by CPI already priced. These two live, near-term drivers (labor vs inflation) roughly offset each other on slope direction, while the actual slope measurement itself says "stalled, no trend." Given the primary slope signal has gone flat and the supporting long-end catalysts (term premium, balance sheet) have both weakened, I no longer have adequate evidence to hold a full steepener. I'm cutting size toward near-neutral, keeping only a small residual steepening tilt given labor's directional lean, rather than reversing outright.

Last meeting I ran a mild steepener (long DGS10, short DGS2, small conviction) built on rising labor slack + shrinking balance sheet + slight steepening drift in slope_2s10s. This meeting's panel materially weakens that case. curve_slope itself has downgraded from "up 0.30" to "flat 0.25": the push above 0.58 failed and settled back to 0.55, 63d change is now negative — the direct measurement of the thing I trade says consolidation, not steepening. That's the single most trade-relevant read since it's literally my instrument.

On the front end, labor_tightness is the one live, repriced signal: unemployment hit a fresh 12-month high (4.50%, range_position 1.00), monotonic 3-month rise, Sahm-style gap widening. This is a real, near-term labor deterioration that should pull front-end yields down as cut expectations build — a genuine steepening force if the long end doesn't follow. But the long end is not cooperating: term_premium shows dgs10 near 12-month lows (range_position 0.06) with 63d/252d solidly negative — the long end has already richened sharply, more than offsetting front-end room. Since 21d for term_premium just barely turned positive (stalling), and balance_sheet's actual data still shows uninterrupted runoff (bearish for duration only when it ends, not yet reflected), the long end has scope to drift either way but is not screaming higher.

Net: front end easing (labor-driven) and long end anchored/rich cancel much of the steepening thesis. The slope's own read (flat) corroborates this reconciliation better than extrapolating from labor or term premium alone. I'm cutting conviction sharply and shrinking the steepener toward near-flat, keeping only a token bias given labor's genuine repricing.

Momentum measures are mixed: headline_3m_annualized (3.43%) still runs hotter than the y/y print, suggesting some upward drift risk, but core_3m_annualized (2.40%) is decelerating and core y/y has actually ticked down 0.08pp over three months, with the latest print (2.83) below the recent local peak (2.91). Core running below headline (core_minus_headline -0.17pp) and decelerating is the more important signal for persistence, and it argues against imminent reacceleration in headline. Net: headline is near a local plateau at an elevated level (0.8-1.0pp above target on core), with recent momentum roughly flat-to-slightly-up on headline but cooling on core. I don't see enough evidence for a confident directional break either way — most likely essentially unchanged next print, perhaps a marginal uptick given the 3m annualized skew.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or headline y/y above 3.15% would signal renewed acceleration; a drop below 2.85% y/y would confirm deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.40% from 4.50%, a decline of 0.10pp — this is the "below 4.60% acceleration threshold" and close to my stated stabilization marker (4.30% or below), though it didn't quite reach that level. My prior "up" call was directionally too aggressive for this print; the rate actually ticked down last month rather than continuing to rise. That said, the broader trend over 12 months is still up (+0.20pp), the 3-month change is still positive (+0.10pp), and the level remains 0.40pp above the 12-month low and 0.40pp above the natural rate estimate, with the Sahm gap holding at 0.43pp — closer to conventional recession-signal territory than to full stabilization. Policy language changes are notable: officials now describe downside employment risks as having "risen in recent months" (upgraded from a static risk assessment), and note "more recent indicators are consistent with" the slowing/upward-edging narrative — suggesting they see continued softening beneath the single-month dip. Given the single-month decline could be noise around a still-elevated and drifting-up base, and the policy tone leans toward further loosening, I lean toward another modest uptick or at least sideways-to-up next report, but with reduced conviction since the immediate print surprised to the downside.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 4.30% would confirm stabilization/reversal; a print above 4.60% would confirm renewed sharp acceleration.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has drifted sideways-to-slightly-up over the last month, closing at 4.18%, matching its recent high in this window. My prior "down" call was wrong in direction (though small in magnitude): the 21d change is now +0.09pp and the yield sits at the top of its recent range, not breaking lower toward 3.95 as I'd flagged. The 12m range position (0.26) shows yield still in the lower third of its 12-month band, but the short-term momentum is modestly up. The 30s10s slope at 0.66pp has not cleared my 0.70 steepening threshold, so the strongest upside trigger isn't confirmed, but it's closer than before. Policy language has shifted meaningfully: the Fed ending balance-sheet runoff and now signaling it will conduct purchases of shorter-term Treasuries to maintain reserve levels is a mild net negative for long-end term premium pressure (removes some supply overhang from runoff, and purchases are concentrated short-end, so less direct long-end support). This is a mixed signal — modest upward yield drift on measurements, offset by ballast from the end of QT/runoff. Net: modest, low-conviction lean toward continued slight upward drift or flat, not the down call I made last time.
Would change its mind if: A break below 4.05 with 30s10s flattening toward 0.55 would confirm renewed downward term-premium pressure; a clean push above 4.25 with slope above 0.70 confirms upside.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on pace/size of new short-term Treasury purchases would clarify net duration supply impact on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify if flight-to-quality is suppressing or not suppressing long yields.

arbitrated.notes:
The direct curve_slope read is now the most decisive input: 2s10s has moved from 0.67 to 0.71, with 21d/63d/252d all positive and the spread near its 12-month high (range position 0.96). This is a live, own-driver measurement of the thing I trade, not an inference — and it has strengthened materially since last meeting, crossing well above my prior flip level of 0.60 with positive momentum on both 21d and 63d. That is exactly the falsifier I set last time for flipping toward a real steepener, so I update.

Cross-checking against front-end and long-end drivers: labor_tightness ticked down to 4.40% (a tighter labor print than the prior 4.50%) but the analyst still leans "up" on trend and Sahm gap remains elevated — a genuinely tightening labor market (falling unemployment) would normally support front-end yields staying anchored/rising, which is curve-flattening pressure, but the single-month move is noisy and the analyst's own conviction is modest (0.30) and hedges hard. Term premium is only mildly up (0.30 conviction, "mixed signal") and explicitly flags it's missing the balance_sheet detail on short-term purchase pace — a gap that cuts toward not letting this stale/uncertain long-end read override the direct slope signal. Balance_sheet turning up (QT ending, shorter-term purchases) is front-end supportive/long-end neutral-to-negative for term premium, reinforcing steepening rather than fighting it.

Net: the direct slope measurement, corroborated by a policy backdrop (easing cycle, QT ending, purchases concentrated short-end) that structurally favors front-end anchoring over long-end support, tips this from my prior near-flat stance into a real steepener view. I'm reversing my carried position.

discounted.why: Single-month unemployment dip is noisy; analyst's own conviction low and mixed with rising trend.
Low conviction, mixed signal, and analyst flags it's missing key balance_sheet detail on long end.

arbitrated.falsifier: A drop in 2s10s slope back below 0.65 sustained across multiple readings, or unemployment falling clearly to/below 4.30%, would flip this back toward flat/flattener.

risks.text: Slope print has been choppy (0.73→0.67→0.71) within the uptrend, risking a false steepening signal.
If labor tightening resumes decisively, front-end could rise faster than long end, flattening the curve.
Pace/size of new short-term Treasury purchases unknown; could surprise either direction on front-end supply.
Reversing a carried position on one month's data risks whipsaw if steepening stalls next print.

trade.rationale: Direct slope measurement confirms durable steepening trend near 12m highs, reinforced by QT-ending/front-end-anchoring policy backdrop; reverse prior near-flat stance into a real steepener.

why.curve_slope: Direct 2s10s print rose 0.67→0.71, all momentum windows positive, near 12m highs; primary evidence.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment ticked down to 4.40% but trend/Sahm gap still elevated; noisy, modest weight.
why.term_premium: Mild upward drift in 10y, low conviction, missing balance_sheet detail; secondary support only.
why.balance_sheet: Assets turning up, QT ending, short-term purchases signaled; front-end supportive, reinforces steepening.
